finding: A continuity queue can have a single current head while still containing an unresolved historical fork. Fork evidence must therefore be derived from predecessor-to-successor cardinality, not head count alone, and must remain visible after a later merge record.

verification.method: isolated Node runtime using byte-identical source and test content

claim_states.observed: Recent continuity records explicitly preserve fork detection as unresolved work; the committed detector and test now implement that check.
claim_states.inferred: Counting only current heads is insufficient because a reconciliation record can hide the fact that two sibling records once diverged from the same predecessor.
claim_states.proposed: Compose normalization, inherited-unresolved validation, and fork detection into one report-only repository inventory before CI enforcement.
claim_states.superseded: Treating a single current queue head as proof that queue history is linear.
unresolved: repository-wide discovery of every M-* record
classification of intentional versus accidental forks

privacy_boundary: Public repository metadata, abstract queue semantics, and synthetic test records only. No private chat text, health or pet-health material, credentials, personal identifiers, or unreviewed uploads were included.
falsification_route: Supply a record set with two successors sharing one predecessor. The detector must report the predecessor and both successors even if a later record depends on both branches; failure to do so falsifies the implementation claim.
